experiments.title: How Adding a One-Time Gift Option to a Slide-Up Promoting Recurring Giving Impacted Transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a one-time gift option to the slide-up will lead to more transactions overall, while maintaining a similar number of sustaining gifts. This approach is designed to attract individuals who may not be ready to commit to a sustaining gift, while still capturing those who were already inclined to make a sustaining contribution.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for a public radio station serving North Carolina, during their February Fund Drive. We aimed to test two different slide-ups: one was a previous version that encouraged prospects to make a sustaining gift, while in the test version, we added an option for a one-time gift to see if it would result in more overall gifts, including sustaining contributions.
Everything else remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In conclusion, the results of this experiment indicate that the control version, which emphasized monthly sustaining donations, outperformed the treatment version, which included both one-time and sustaining gift options. The control version generated 53 gifts and $1,510 in revenue, compared to the treatment version's 23 gifts and $595 in revenue.
Breaking down the results further:
The control version had 15 one-time gifts and 38 recurring gifts.
 	The treatment version had 6 one-time gifts and 17 recurring gifts.
 	The control version also saw more first-time gifts, with 35 first-time gifts compared to the treatment's 13.
A key finding was that the control version resulted in a 58% higher donation volume across all traffic than the treatment version, with 100% statistical confidence. This suggests that focusing solely on sustaining donations was more effective at driving contributions, as adding a one-time gift option did not increase the total number of donations.
Additionally, four sustainers made another one-time donation, all through the control version.
Moving forward, it would be beneficial to continue emphasizing the importance and impact of monthly sustaining gifts, as this approach appears to resonate more effectively with prospects. Further testing could explore different messaging strategies and donation options to identify the most effective combination for future fundraising campaigns. These insights will be key to optimizing the approach to maximizing donations in the future.

experiments.title: Can a homepage overlay outperform a full redirect for year-end fundraising?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that using an on-page overlay takeover with a donation form for all homepage visitors would achieve higher conversion rates than a full-page redirect because preserving user context and choice would reduce bounce and resistance associated with forced interruption.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2025 - 01/01/2026
WUNC is a public media organization serving central North Carolina, relying on community support to fund independent journalism, local storytelling, and regional news coverage. As part of its 2025 growth goals, WUNC is focused on increasing digital fundraising performance during high-urgency moments while protecting audience trust and brand experience.
This experiment ran on the WUNC homepage during the final four days of the year—its highest-impact fundraising window—to test two different homepage takeover approaches. The team compared a full-page redirect to a donation form against an on-page overlay featuring a donation form or gift array with supporting year-end messaging. The test was prioritized to inform long-term homepage fundraising strategy, specifically whether urgency-driven interruption or contextual choice better aligns with WUNC’s audience expectations during major drives.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Big takeaway:

The homepage redirect significantly outperformed the overlay on primary conversion rate, winning with 99% statistical confidence. While the overlay experience generated more total downstream donations overall, it underperformed on direct donation transactions during the experiment window.
📊 Performance Metrics (Primary KPI)
Conversion rate (donations / samples):
Redirect: 78 / 1,323 = 5.9%
 	Overlay: 44 / 1,491 = 3.0%
 	Result: Redirect wins at 99% confidence (two-proportion z-test)
✂️ Donor Type Breakdowns (Directional, not statistically significant)
Sustainer conversion rate:
Redirect: 6 / 1,323 = 0.45%

 	Confidence: Not statistically significant (low sample size)
One-time donor conversion rate:
Redirect: 72 / 1,323 = 5.4%
 	Overlay: 40 / 1,491 = 2.7%
 	Confidence: ~99% (driven by overall transaction volume)
First-time donor conversion rate:
Redirect: 24 / 1,323 = 1.8%
 	Overlay: 9 / 1,491 = 0.6%
 	Confidence: ~98–99% (directionally strong, but smaller n)
📎 Additional Context (Not Primary KPI)
Revenue:
 	Redirect: $25,285
 	Overlay: $10,375
 	Downstream donations from segment (not direct experiment click):
 	Overlay: 85
 	Redirect: 78

experiments.title: Does concise donation form copy outperform long-form messaging?
experiments.researchQuestion: If we shorten and clarify the donation form copy to present a concise value proposition, then more donors will complete their gifts (conversion rate increase) and potentially give at higher levels (revenue increase). This is because WUNC’s multichannel donors are already primed and don’t need long explanations—clear, streamlined copy will reduce friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2025 - 10/06/2025
This experiment was conducted on WUNC’s primary donation form, which receives the majority of donor traffic from email, direct navigation, and on-site promotions. The goal was to evaluate whether shorter, more action-oriented copy would improve donor conversion rates compared to WUNC’s traditional, longer-form messaging.
WUNC’s standard donation form historically emphasized emotional storytelling, a sense of community, and shared responsibility for supporting public radio in North Carolina. However, recent research in donation behavior and user experience has suggested that minimizing friction—especially in digital donation flows—can lead to higher conversion rates. Internally, there was interest in exploring whether this applied to WUNC’s audience, especially during high-engagement periods.
The team hypothesized that short, punchy copy emphasizing urgency and clarity might increase conversion by reducing cognitive load and decision-making time. However, there was concern that cutting too much might sacrifice the warmth, trust, and shared identity that WUNC’s donors often cite as core motivations for giving.

experiments.title: How a slide up with a gift array performs against a pop-up with a simple donate button
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering people specific gift asks for the fund drive will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2025 - 08/13/2025
During a special fund drive for OPB, the public media broadcasting station for Oregon and Southwest Washington, we wanted to test how a standard pop-up with a simple donate button (what OPB traditionally uses during a fund drive) would perform against a slide-up that included specific gift arrays.
This test was run on article pages only; a separate test was run on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There were some exciting results from this experiment. The unique fundraising environment for this test is not something that cannot be easily repeatable, unless there is another urgent/emergency campaign. That being said, the increase in conversion rate from the slide up gift array version is huge and statistically valid. The results above can be directly attributed to people clicking the button/array and completing their gift.

A couple of interesting downstream results to be aware of:
The control version had a slightly higher average gift than the treatment version; given the lower volume of gifts on the control, that could be the influence of a larger gift or two, or the fact that we were showing donors a gift array up front in the treatment version, leading them to give a lower average gift (gift arrays on the actual donation forms were the same for both control and treatment).
 	Regarding the overall influence both had on total giving, the gift array version had a slightly larger influence, showing a 23% increase in conversion rates overall with a 96% level of confidence. This means that people who saw the gift array version and then made a gift at some point to a donation form on the website (not necessarily the one linked directly from the pop-up) were 23% more likely to convert.

experiments.title: How a donation form with an in-line payment module performs against a pop-up module.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a in-line payment module for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion dates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2025 - 07/01/2025
This organization’s donation platform launched a new structure to their main donation form (due to enhanced PCI compliance) where the billing info would pop-up in a new screen once you chose your gift array. We wanted to test whether this potential new layer of friction impacted donation conversion rates, so we created a more standard donation form as the treatment variant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While our original hypothesis was that adding the pop-up would increase friction, and therefore reduce conversion rates, the results of this experiment showed the opposite.

Not only the control in this case have a 32% decrease in conversions with a 100% level of confidence, but those results also translated when looking at one-time versus monthly gifts. The control had a 29% decrease in conversions for one-time gifts, and a 34% decrease in monthly gifts, both with 100% level of confidence.

For future experiments, we will want to test whether the placement of the module on mobile made any difference, as well as the language used to 'click' to get to the payment information.

experiments.title: Does Direct Messaging Increase Donor Conversion During High-Urgency Campaigns?
experiments.researchQuestion: The direct messaging treatment will drive higher conversion rates by reducing ambiguity and increasing perceived urgency; individuals are more likely to take action when the problem and required response are stated explicitly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2025 - 05/01/2025
This experiment was conducted by an NPR member station serving listeners across North Carolina, during their Spring Fund Drive. The campaign took place during a period of elevated urgency, as potential federal funding cuts were in the news cycle and top of mind for many supporters.
The goal of the experiment was to test the impact of message explicitness on donor engagement. We compared two versions of campaign messaging: the control featured a more indirect, suggestive tone, implying the risk to public media funding without naming specific actors. In contrast, the treatment used more direct, unambiguous language, explicitly referencing Congress and the political threats to public media funding.
While the tone and framing of the email copy differed, both versions directed users to the same donation landing page. This allowed us to isolate the effect of message directness on donor behavior, particularly under conditions of heightened urgency and media salience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This messaging experiment, run during a period of heightened urgency around public media funding, tested two approaches:
A Funding Version with direct, issue-specific language referencing Congressional threats, and
 	A General Version with a softer, more implied message.
Key Learnings:
The Funding Version drove nearly double the total attributed purchases (125 vs. 67), including post-view conversions, indicating a stronger overall persuasive effect.
 	Both versions saw similar direct donor counts (11 vs. 10), but the Funding Version's stronger post-view performance suggests it had a more lasting cognitive impact—viewers retained the message and returned later to donate, even without clicking.
 	Despite lower click volume, the Funding Version likely attracted more qualified or high-intent users—those who clicked were more likely to convert, contributing to a lower cost per donor ($54.22 vs. $59.62).
 	The alignment between the Funding Version’s direct messaging and the donation page content may have created a more seamless and cognitively consistent user experience, reducing friction and increasing likelihood to give.
 	Although the General Version received more clicks overall, these users were less likely to donate, highlighting the importance of click quality over quantity.
Takeaway:
Lean into Direct, Issue-Oriented Messaging
Test Message-to-Landing Page Alignment
Explore New Creative to Expand Reach
new creative formats
Differentiate Messaging by Audience Segment
Analyze Post-View Conversion Behavior

experiments.title: Does a Humanized Video Outperform Traditional Copy?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the version with the video will outperform the traditional body copy because video has the potential to create a more engaging, emotional connection with the audience. By incorporating visuals, voice, and storytelling, the video humanizes WUNC’s mission, making the impact of public radio feel more personal and immediate. This humanized approach is expected to deepen emotional resonance, create a stronger sense of urgency, and ultimately lead to more transactions during the fund drive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2025 - 04/29/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, an NPR member station serving listeners throughout North Carolina, during their Spring Fund Drive. In previous campaigns, we’ve tested a variety of approaches using a slide-up donation prompt featuring a gift array. For this particular test, we wanted to explore whether incorporating a video in place of traditional body copy would lead to more transactions. The structure of the slide-up remained largely unchanged—donors still saw the same headline and the same suggested gift array amounts. The only variable was the presence of the video in one version, compared to body text in the other. This allowed us to isolate the impact of the video and evaluate whether visual storytelling could better drive engagement and giving behavior.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The control version with body copy outperformed the video treatment in both total gifts and total revenue. The control generated 70 total gifts and raised $3,078, compared to 53 gifts and $1,986 raised by the treatment. Sustaining gifts were higher in the control (46 vs. 38), and it also brought in more first-time sustaining donors (37 vs. 26). However, the video treatment did result in a slightly higher total from sustaining gifts ($851 vs. $813) and attracted more first-time one-time donors (10 vs. 6). While the video showed some promise in reaching brand-new donors, especially one-time givers, it ultimately did not surpass the traditional copy in overall donor response or revenue generation.

Suggested Next Steps:

Refine Video Length and Content: Test shorter, more focused videos or versions that directly highlight donor impact, urgency, or testimonials. It’s possible the current video didn’t clearly resonate with the majority of prospects.
Test Hybrid Messaging: Try combining a short video with a concise supporting line or two of body copy to guide the viewer and reinforce the CTA.
 	Segmented Testing: Run the video test only for new visitors or new users, where the video’s ability to humanize WUNC might have more impact.
 	Button Placement and CTA Testing: Explore different placements or phrasings for the donate button within the slide-up to make it more action-oriented or prominent
 	Follow-Up Engagement Test: Test using the video slide-up not as the initial ask, but as a retargeting element for users who didn’t give on their first visit.

experiments.title: How localizing Facebook creative impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that localizing the Facebook ad creative to directly address the target audience and incorporating a clear call to action will increase the rate of completed registrations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2024 - 08/30/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a radio station based in North Carolina, involved a targeted Facebook media campaign designed to evaluate a novel approach. Our objective was to determine whether localizing the ad creative and directly addressing the target audience, coupled with a clear call to action, would enhance the rate of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Localizing the Facebook ad creative to directly address the target audience and incorporating a clear call to action significantly increased the rate of completed registrations by 51.5% for all traffic with a 97.4% level of confidence. This indicates that personalized and targeted messaging can have a significant impact on the desired outcome.

2. The Cost per Click increased by 157%, indicating that while the targeted approach may have increased engagement, it also came at a higher cost. However, the overall decrease in Cost per Registration by 352% suggests that the investment in targeted advertising paid off in terms of efficiency and effectiveness.

3. Female visitors showed a 518% increase in completed registrations with a 97% level of confidence, indicating that the personalized messaging resonated particularly well with this demographic group. This highlights the importance of understanding and tailoring marketing efforts to specific audience segments.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue leveraging personalized and targeted messaging to engage with the audience effectively. By understanding the preferences and behaviors of different demographic groups, such as females in this case, marketing strategies can be optimized to drive better results. Additionally, monitoring and analyzing key metrics such as Cost per Click and Cost per Registration can provide insights into the efficiency and effectiveness of advertising campaigns, allowing for continuous improvement and optimization.

experiments.title: How targeting a specific audience effected click-through rates on a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe targeting a specific audience on Facebook ads leads to higher click-through rates due to increased ad relevance, reduced ad fatigue, and enhanced personalization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2024 - 08/30/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, utilized a paid Facebook media campaign to test a new approach. Our goal was to determine whether tailoring our creative and ad copy to target Facebook users with an interest in health and wellness would lead to higher click-through rates and an increased number of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. **Targeting a specific audience leads to higher click-through rates**: The experiment showed that personalizing the Facebook ad creative and copy to target an audience interested in health resulted in a 153.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. This demonstrates that targeting a specific audience can significantly improve the effectiveness of ad campaigns.
2. **Significant improvements in key metrics**: The treatment group not only saw a substantial increase in Clicks for All Traffic but also experienced a 453.9% increase in Complete Registrations. This indicates that by targeting a specific audience, not only can you drive more traffic but also increase the likelihood of conversions.
3. **Segment-specific improvements**: The experiment also revealed segment-specific improvements, such as a 342.4% increase in clicks for the 55-64 age group, a 71.4% increase for visitors aged 65 and above, a 194.6% increase for Mobile visitors, and a 137.4% increase for Female visitors. This underscores the importance of audience segmentation in ad targeting.

experiments.keyLearnings:

It may also be beneficial to further analyze the cost implications of these creative changes and consider optimizing for a balance between cost per click and cost per registration.
Overall, the key learning from this experiment is that creative design plays a crucial role in driving user engagement and registration. By leveraging authentic and eye-catching visuals, nonprofits like WUNC can effectively attract and convert their target audience.

This approach aims to instill a sense of time sensitivity among prospects, emphasizing the relevance of acquiring this information in anticipation of the election year.
experiments.summary:

This experiment was conducted on behalf of WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, using a paid media channel on Facebook. The ad copy promotes an email series content offer providing prospects with information on elections and politics. Our objective was to determine whether adding urgency to the copy would increase the rate of complete registrations. Both the control and treatment groups utilized the same creative elements and call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings:

1. Incorporating urgency into ad copy can significantly increase completion rates for registrations.
2. The treatment group, which included urgency in the ad copy, saw a 46.8% increase in emails acquired for all traffic compared to the control group.
3. The results were statistically significant with a 96.3% confidence level.
4. This approach successfully instilled a sense of time sensitivity among prospects, emphasizing the relevance of the information provided in the email series in anticipation of the election year.
In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of urgency in ad copy across different campaigns and audiences. Additionally, we can explore other ways to create a sense of urgency, such as limited-time offers or exclusive content. It is important to analyze the impact of urgency on different metrics besides email acquisition, such as engagement and conversion rates, to further optimize our marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How an “Organic Status” Facebook Ad Affected Conversion Rate

rows.name: NA | The People's Pharmacy Cookbook Control
NA | The People's Pharmacy Cookbook Treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an “Organic Status” ad for Facebook users will achieve an increased conversion rate because  the ad will stand out amongst other sponsored posts..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/10/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station. We decided to test if an organic looking facebook post used as an ad increased clicks and conversion rates compared to their traditional looking ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning in this experiment is that Facebook users are more likely to click on an ad if it seems like it is an organic post. Because the ad looks like an organic status post, it stands out to users, so they don't immediately assume it's an ad and scroll past it. Making the ad stand out more to users increased the number of clicks by 49.5% with a 99.9% Level of Confidence & decreased the Cost per Click by 19.6% with a 99.9% Level of Confidence as well. I believe this positive effect was also impacted by the fact that the treatment seemed like a personal recommendation versus just a promotion of a product. It is important to note that these ads ran exclusively in-feed on Facebook. All other ad placements were turned off.
It is also interesting to note that the older the audience, the higher the conversion rate. This could be due to the fact that an older user may not be able to tell as easily a a younger user that it is an ad and not an organic post making them more likely to click on it.
Moving forward, we should continue to test this type of "Organic Status" ad on various types on content offers that appeal to varying types and ages of audiences. We should also try applying the strategy of making an ad look more organic in other ways, such as a normal status post, and maybe try an organic looking Instagram & Facebook story post as well.

experiments.title: Can democracy-focused messaging outperform urgency-based copy on a donation page during EOY?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that using EOY-reframed, democracy- and community-focused messaging for WUNC donors would increase donor motivation and conversion compared to crisis-focused copy because values-based framing can strengthen intrinsic motivation and reinforce donors’ identity as supporters of public media.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2025 - 11/25/2025
WUNC is a public media organization serving North Carolina with a mission to inform, engage, and strengthen civic life, supported largely by listener contributions. As the organization entered the 2025 end-of-year (EOY) fundraising period, sustaining revenue growth and donor motivation remained key program goals.
This experiment tested donation page copy during a high-traffic EOY moment to understand whether reframing the ask around democracy and community impact could outperform WUNC’s existing urgency-based messaging tied to federal funding cuts. The test was prioritized as part of a broader effort to evolve messaging beyond crisis framing and assess when values-based appeals resonate most with donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings
Big takeaway: The urgency-based control outperformed the democracy-focused treatment on the primary KPI (donations). Secondary metrics show a similar directional pattern, though only one reaches near-significant confidence.
📊 Performance metrics (with confidence comparisons)
Total donations (primary KPI)Control: 25 / 126 (19.8%)Treatment: 13 / 111 (11.7%)Difference: +8.1 pts favoring controlConfidence level: ~92%❌ Not statistically valid at 95%, but directionally meaningfulOne-time donationsControl: 19 / 126 (15.1%)Treatment: 8 / 111 (7.2%)Difference: +7.9 pts favoring controlConfidence level: ~95%⚠️ Borderline statistical validity; strongest secondary signal in favor of urgency messagingSustaining donationsControl: 6 / 126 (4.8%)Treatment: 5 / 111 (4.5%)Difference: +0.3 ptsConfidence level: <10%❌ No meaningful or directional differenceRevenueControl: $4,100Treatment: $880Revenue strongly favored the control, driven by higher conversion volume.⚠️ No statistical confidence calculated, as revenue is not suitable for a two-proportion z-test in this context.
✂️ Segment interpretation
The performance gap was driven primarily by one-time donor conversion, not sustaining behavior.Sustainer response was effectively neutral between variants.
Next Steps
Continue running with the current urgency-based messaging for EOY. Additional messaging tests are planned to determine when donor responsiveness begins shifting toward values-based framing. Timing is a key variable under consideration, as this test ran immediately before Giving Tuesday, when urgency messaging historically performs better.

experiments.title: Can Changing the Way We Frame a Newsletter Impact Sign-Ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe framing the newsletter as a trusted filter in a fast-changing world will increase conversions by tapping into uncertainty avoidance and information overload.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/29/2025 - 07/19/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, an NPR member station serving listeners throughout North Carolina. The test focused on the landing page for WUNC’s “What’s Trending” newsletter. We wanted to determine whether reframing the newsletter copy—as a trusted filter in a fast-changing world—would lead to an increase in email sign-ups. All other elements of the landing page remained unchanged.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment
Framing Boosted Sign-Ups
Reframing the newsletter copy to position it as a trusted filter in a fast-changing world led to a 41.1% increase in email sign-ups.
Statistical Confidence
The result had a 90.1% confidence level, suggesting a strong signal, though additional testing is recommended to confirm validity.
Gift Size Observation
Revenue performance was observed as a 1-to-1 gift comparison, with the treatment receiving a substantially larger gift. However, this is anecdotal and not sufficient to conclude a higher average gift resulted from the treatment.
Hypothesis Validation
The experiment supported the hypothesis that appealing to uncertainty avoidance and information overload can effectively drive newsletter sign-ups.
Recommendations for Future Testing
Test Messaging Themes That Address Emotional or Cognitive Needs
Building on the success of the “trusted filter” framing, explore other emotionally resonant themes—such as reliability, belonging, or empowerment—to identify which underlying motivations most effectively drive sign-ups.
Experiment with Messaging Hierarchy and Presentation
Evaluate how the placement, tone, and format of key messaging (e.g., headlines vs. supporting copy, minimalist vs. detailed explanations) influence conversion rates on acquisition pages.
Extend Testing Across Related Channels and Entry Points
Apply and test winning messaging strategies on other digital touchpoints—such as social media ads, on-site pop-ups, or podcast mentions—to assess consistency and scalability in email acquisition performance.

experiments.title: Can Introducing a Slide-Up Prompt Donations from Prospective Donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a slide-up donation prompt to the high-traffic “Ways to Listen” page will increase conversions by reaching engaged users at a moment of intent without disrupting their experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 06/09/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, an NPR member station serving listeners across North Carolina. The goal was to explore whether introducing a slide-up donation prompt on the station’s “Ways to Listen” page could increase conversions.
To do this, the team developed a new web asset specifically for this high-traffic page. The slide-up was designed to engage users with a subtle, timely prompt that encourages giving—without interrupting their browsing experience. By targeting a page that typically serves an informational (rather than fundraising) purpose, the test aims to uncover a new opportunity to engage potential donors in a low-friction, high-visibility context.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Conclusion & Next Steps

The slide-up treatment increased conversions from 0.07% to 0.27%, a 297% relative lift. While the confidence level of 81.9% does not meet the threshold for statistical significance, the notable increase suggests that the slide-up is an effective way to engage visitors on the “Ways to Listen” page—a section typically focused on information rather than fundraising.

Key Learnings:
The “Ways to Listen” page presents an untapped opportunity to introduce donation asks in a non-intrusive way.
 	Visitors on this page are open to engaging with donation prompts when presented subtly through slide-ups.
 	Even a simple slide-up design can significantly improve conversion rates over no prompt.
Recommendations for Next Steps:
Design Test: Introduce a more humanized slide-up featuring a photo of a popular WUNC host to build trust and emotional connection.
 	Messaging Test: Experiment with different copy that emphasizes the listener’s impact or the value of WUNC’s programming to increase motivation.
 	CTA Test: Try varying calls to action (e.g., “Support WUNC,” “Join Our Community,” “Help Keep Us On Air”) to find the most compelling phrasing.

experiments.title: Does a pop-up with future framing copy generate more donations than a contrast effect copy?
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believed that emphasizing urgency and the challenges ahead would better motivate donations than the paywall-focused message, as it could create a stronger emotional incentive and sense of immediacy for users.
Psychological Framing
This test compared two psychological tactics:
Future Framing (Test): Highlights what lies ahead—upcoming threats, opportunities, or needs. This approach encourages users to act now to influence future outcomes.
Contrast Effect (Control): Draws attention to what users currently have (free access) and implies a risk of losing it. This technique relies on juxtaposing the current benefit with the potential consequences of inaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2025 - 04/01/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the impact of homepage pop-up messaging on donor acquisition. The goal was to determine whether leaning into urgency and future challenges would outperform messaging focused on WUNC’s lack of a paywall.

experiments.title: How donor autonomy language on active donor impacts transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating donor autonomy language in our fundraising emails targeted at the active donor segment will lead to an increase in donations. By emphasizing the donor’s sense of choice and control, we expect to empower recipients, making them feel more confident and motivated to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted in partnership with WUNC, a North Carolina NPR station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The primary goal of this study was to explore the impact of donor autonomy language on behavior and engagement.
The experiment focused on a series of five emails sent to an active segment of WUNC’s donor base. Each email aimed to evaluate whether emphasizing donor autonomy—language that reinforces a donor’s sense of choice and control over their giving—would influence donation rates and overall responsiveness.
To measure the effect, we created two variations of the emails. The control group received messages without any autonomy-focused language, while the test group received emails that intentionally incorporated phrases designed to highlight donor choice and empowerment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment demonstrated that incorporating donor autonomy language did not lead to improved conversion rates among active donors. Despite both the control and treatment emails generating the same number of clicks (42 each), the control group consistently outperformed the treatment group in donation conversions, achieving a 0.44% conversion rate compared to the treatment’s 0.26% conversion rate—a 40.4% decrease.

The control group saw higher performance in all email sends except for the third email (as seen above), where the treatment briefly outperformed the control with 6 donations compared to 3. However, this isolated success was not enough to offset the overall underperformance of the treatment.

These findings suggest that donor autonomy language, as tested in this campaign, may not resonate as strongly with active donors and could potentially detract from conversion effectiveness in this context. Further testing may be required to refine the messaging approach or explore other engagement strategies.

experiments.title: How Donor Autonomy Language on Lapsed Donor Impacts Transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating donor autonomy language in fundraising emails targeted at a lapsed donor segment will re-engage recipients by emphasizing their sense of choice and control. This approach is expected to make lapsed donors feel more empowered and valued, ultimately increasing donation conversions and encouraging them to renew their support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted in partnership with WUNC, a North Carolina NPR station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The primary goal of this study was to explore the impact of donor autonomy language on behavior and engagement.
The experiment focused on a series of five emails sent to a lapsed segment of WUNC’s donor base. Each email aimed to evaluate whether emphasizing donor autonomy—language that reinforces a donor’s sense of choice and control over their giving—would influence donation rates and overall responsiveness.
To measure the effect, we created two variations of the emails. The control group received messages without any autonomy-focused language, while the test group received emails that intentionally incorporated phrases designed to highlight donor choice and empowerment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In the lapsed donor segment, incorporating donor autonomy language in the treatment emails resulted in a significant improvement in conversion rates compared to the control. While the control group had a conversion rate of 0.01%, the treatment group achieved 0.07%—a 398.4% relative increase with 89.7% confidence.

Although the control group generated slightly more clicks (44 vs. 39), the treatment group consistently outperformed the control in donations across all five emails. This suggests that donor autonomy language may be more effective at re-engaging lapsed donors, potentially resonating with their desire for choice and control as they consider renewing their support.

These results highlight an opportunity to further explore and optimize autonomy-driven messaging for reactivating lapsed donors.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts lapsed donors during a fund drive

Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates. This specific experiment targeted lapsed donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:

- The experiment aimed to test the impact of reinforcing the value proposition for lapsed donors in an email during WUNC's Fall Fund Drive.
- The treatment group, which received the additional call-out reinforcing WUNC's value proposition, showed a significant increase in clicks for all traffic, with an 81.8% improvement.
- The statistical level of confidence was 89.5%, indicating that the results are approaching validity, but further testing is recommended for certainty.
-Moreover, while there were only 3 gifts total, the addition of the value prop did increase conversion rates by 100%.

This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to test new copy on their primary donation page. Our goal was to assess whether incorporating language that emphasizes North Carolina and focuses on the donor’s personal connection would impact donation levels.

3 . experiments.title: How humanizing Facebook Creative impacts complete registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Utilizing targeted imagery increases engagement and downloads by helping Facebook audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook. It fosters a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2024
This study was conducted on Facebook for WUNC, a public radio station in North Carolina, to optimize click-through rates and enhance overall conversions for a promotional campaign. The experiment aimed to refine the effectiveness of an advertisement for an eBook about outdoor activities in North Carolina. The experimental design compared the performance of an ad featuring the eBook’s cover image against an alternative ad featuring a person engaged in outdoor recreation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on optimizing click-through rates and conversions for an eBook promotion are as follows:

1. The treatment, which featured an ad with targeted imagery of a person engaged in outdoor activities, resulted in a 35.0% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control ad with the eBook's cover image.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 92.6%, indicating that the effectiveness of the treatment is approaching validity but further testing would be necessary to confirm its impact.

3. The hypothesis that targeted imagery helps audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook, fostering a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates, was supported by the results of the experiment.

Moving forward, future experimentation should consider the following points:

1. Continue to test and refine the use of targeted imagery in advertisements to enhance engagement and conversions. Further experiments can help strengthen the validity of the findings and optimize the effectiveness of promotional campaigns.

2. Explore different types of imagery and messaging to determine which combinations resonate best with the target audience. This can inform future advertising strategies and improve campaign performance.

3. Implement A/B testing on a larger scale or over an extended period to gather more data and increase the confidence level of results. This will provide a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of different ad elements on audience behavior.

Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that targeted imagery can have a significant effect on engagement and conversions. By leveraging this insight and continuing to test and refine strategies, WUNC can enhance the effectiveness of its promotional campaigns and drive better outcomes for future eBook promotions and other marketing efforts.

experiments.title: How humanizing an Anti-Paywall pop-up effects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that integrating an anti-paywall pop-up onto the WUNC homepage will inspire prospects to consider donating. By presenting them with a clear value proposition by an actual person and reminding them of WUNC’s commitment to providing free content, we aim to instill a sense of reciprocity and encourage support for the station.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2024 - 06/24/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to assess the impact of implementing an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the homepage for new website visitors. The primary goal was to determine if this approach could effectively boost donation rates by immediately engaging visitors upon entry and potentially fostering greater support for WUNC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the implementation of an anti-paywall pop-up featuring a personalized message and photo of an employee on the WUNC homepage did not lead to an increase in donation rates. In fact, the treatment group actually saw a 21.3% decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence for these results is 84.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.
Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. It is important to continue experimenting with different strategies and approaches to engage website visitors and encourage donations. Future experiments should focus on testing a variety of tactics, such as different messaging, timing of pop-ups, and targeting specific segments of website visitors, to determine what truly resonates and drives donation conversions. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure a high level of statistical confidence in the results before drawing conclusions and making decisions based on the findings of any experiment.

experiments.title: Does Humanizing Facebook Creative Improve Click-Through-Rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: Utilizing targeted imagery increases engagement and downloads by helping Facebook audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook. It fosters a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/30/2024
This study was conducted on Facebook for WUNC, a public radio station in North Carolina, to optimize click-through rates and enhance overall conversions for a promotional campaign. The experiment aimed to refine the effectiveness of an advertisement for an eBook about outdoor activities in North Carolina. The experimental design compared the performance of an ad featuring the eBook’s cover image against an alternative ad featuring a person engaged in outdoor recreation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that utilizing targeted imagery, specifically featuring a person engaged in outdoor activities, can significantly increase engagement and conversions for promotional campaigns such as eBooks. In this case, the treatment ad featuring a person saw a 12.7% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control ad featuring the eBook cover image, with a high level of statistical confidence at 95.2%.
These results suggest that incorporating images of people enjoying the outdoor activities showcased in the eBook can help the audience visualize themselves benefiting from the content, thus fostering a stronger connection and driving action. In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using targeted imagery that resonates with the target audience to enhance engagement and conversion rates. Additionally, it could be beneficial to further test different types of imagery and messaging to continuously optimize the effectiveness of promotional campaigns.

experiments.title: How testing gift images in a dear reader helps influence gifts during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding images of the thank you gifts for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates because people want to see the gifts they will be getting as a thank-you for their donation..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 06/04/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. During their spring pledge drive, we wanted to run a 3-way test to see whether promoting their premium thank-you gifts using a dear reader style placement would help influence more donations.
We’ve tested dear reader asks in the past, with little lift in direct donations. So this test was designed to see whether having the images, versus not (and no-dear reader at all) would help influence overall online donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not see a lot of direct donations that can be attributed to the dear readers, we did see a slight lift approaching a valid level of confidence by using the dear reader overall, with a higher lift in donor conversion rates through using the images of potential thank you gifts.

Further testing of this concept would test the placement of the dear reader, as well as strengthening the language and making the connection between those reading the news articles and the 'why' they need to donate to support the work of WUNC.

experiments.title: How personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality impacted donations during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality for pledge donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/04/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. Since a lot of web traffic during pledge drives is driven by offline sources (mainly radio) we wanted to see whether personalizing a sticky bar with a quote/image from a specific on-air personality, who would be on the air during a specific time and day, would make an impact on overall online donations.
We ran this as a 3-way test, with a control, sticky bar with quote but no image, and sticky bar with quote and image of on-air personality.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test whether personalizing a sticky bar with a specific on-air personality would impact overall online donations for WUNC, a nonprofit NPR station. The treatment showed a 13.4% increase in donations for all traffic, with an 87.6% statistical level of confidence, indicating that personalizing the sticky bar had a positive effect on donor conversion rates. However, the results are not entirely valid and require further testing to ensure the intended impact.
While results varied from day-to-day, the overall lift that adding a sticky bar to the homepage during the pledge drive was impactful, with an 87% level of confidence that adding one would help increase overall donations.
Interestingly enough, while the image treatment version had a slightly lower conversion rate vis-a-vis the impact on overall online donations, when digging into the directly-attributed results, the treatment version had 6 gifts compared to the no image version of 3 gifts.
Further testing is definitely warranted, as is trying to quantify the connection between offline (radio) and online traffic.

experiments.title: Does adding a credibility block to a content offer increases email acquisition?
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding a credibility block highlighting WUNC’s editorial team to the short-copy landing page will increase email sign-ups (primary KPI) and increase donation conversion rate (secondary KPI) by reinforcing trust and perceived value without changing the streamlined structure that previously improved performance.
experiments.summary:

WUNC tested whether adding a credibility block to the winning short-copy version of the Get Out Guide landing page would impact performance. The short-copy version (problem–solution narrative, streamlined body copy, no bullets) had previously won and became the new control. The test version held all layout and copy constant while introducing a credibility block highlighting WUNC’s reporters and editorial expertise. The goal was to isolate the effect of the credibility element alone.
experiments.keyLearnings:
🧠 Overall Takeaway
This test does not provide sufficient evidence to adopt or remove the credibility block based on performance impact. The directional split between lower email acquisition and higher donation behavior suggests the block may influence downstream trust signals differently than initial sign-up intent, but the data is not statistically reliable enough to support a firm decision.

experiments.title: Can a slide-down donation prompt outperform WUNC’s standard slide-up during CYE?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing a slide-down donation prompt against our standard slide-up for all homepage visitors would help identify whether a fresh, less familiar user experience could improve engagement and conversions during CYE because repeated exposure to the same slide-up format may be leading to creative fatigue, while a new slide-down interaction could draw renewed attention without major structural or messaging changes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/25/2025 - 12/08/2025
WUNC is a public media organization serving central North Carolina, funded primarily through listener and reader support. As part of its 2025 year-end growth goals, WUNC focused on optimizing high-traffic homepage fundraising elements while minimizing risk during its most important revenue window.
This experiment, conducted during the CYE period, tested a modest UX variation to assess whether a refreshed interaction pattern could improve performance without altering the messaging or offer.
The team compared WUNC’s standard slide-up donation prompt, used across multiple drives, to a new slide-down version using the same gift array and copy. The test was prioritized to evaluate potential creative fatigue in the existing unit and to explore low-risk ways to diversify on-site fundraising experiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:

The existing slide-up outperformed the slide-down on donation conversion rate, winning directionally with 78% confidence. Results did not reach conventional statistical significance, and the experiment was ended early to prioritize year-end revenue.

experiments.title: Can shortened, problem–solution copy increase Get Out Guide sign-ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing a shortened version of the Get Out Guide landing page for all visitors would increase email sign-ups because quickly establishing the reader’s problem, clearly presenting WUNC’s solution, and simplifying the layout would improve relevance, readability, and trust, encouraging more visitors to complete the sign-up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2025 - 12/18/2025
WUNC is a public media organization serving central North Carolina, producing local journalism and cultural coverage supported by its audience. As part of its 2025 growth strategy, WUNC is focused on expanding its email audience through content-led offers that introduce new users to its editorial value and create long-term engagement opportunities.
This experiment tested a revised landing page for WUNC’s Get Out Guide, a content offer promoted primarily through Meta ads to both new audiences and existing followers. Building on a previous winning approach from the Education 101 series, the team prioritized this test to evaluate whether shorter, more focused copy could improve email acquisition and downstream donations without changing the core offer. The test compared the existing long-form page with bullet points against a streamlined, problem–solution–driven version designed to improve clarity, flow, and perceived relevance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Big takeaway:

The shortened copy outperformed the long version on both email acquisition volume and conversion rate, but results did not reach statistical significance. Performance was directionally positive and supports further iteration rather than immediate rollout.

experiments.title: Does More Context Increase Email Sign-Ups for Content Offers?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that providing more context and credibility through longer copy—including a testimonial, bullet points, and a detailed explanation—would better persuade users to sign up for the email series by building trust and emotional resonance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/02/2025 - 10/13/2025
This test evaluated the impact of copy length on the Education 101 email series landing page, which offers readers a free series covering public education policy in North Carolina.
The long copy version included:
A quote from the author
A longer headline
Bullet points outlining what users would learn
Contextual background on the reporter and WUNC’s editorial credibility
The short copy version removed these supporting elements and focused directly on the offer. The shorter layout also surfaced the reporter’s bio above the fold on most screen sizes.
Note: While this test was part of a broader effort to improve Meta-driven performance, all traffic in this experiment came from Meta ads. That context is useful when interpreting results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
🔍 Main Takeaway
The shorter copy version generated more email signups, but the difference was not statistically significant (58% confidence). This suggests the difference was likely due to specific copy and layout decisions, not just length alone.
Since the two versions differed in multiple ways—headline, presence of a quote, bullet points, and bio visibility—we cannot isolate what caused the performance lift. This test shows that this specific short version outperformed this specific long version, not that short copy always wins.

experiments.title: Do minimalist ads for newsletter signups improve donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that simplified, headline-style ad creative with a clear call to action would outperform a more visually complex layout in generating donations. The assumption was that complexity could reduce persuasive impact in mission-driven creative by requiring more cognitive effort to understand.

Timeframe: 03/29/2025 - 04/25/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate whether simplified, headline-style ad creative would outperform more complex designs in Meta ads for donor acquisition. The test was inspired by recent behavioral research from the Journal of Consumer Research that found simple, minimalist advertising often outperforms complex designs. While more detailed ads may be perceived as higher effort, simplicity allows messages to be processed more fluently and emotionally, leading to higher engagement.

experiments.title: Does reducing friction on a premium landing page affect conversion rate for Meta Ads?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that removing premium-related fields and focusing the page experience around the donation itself would reduce friction and increase conversion rates and cost efficiency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/08/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate whether reducing friction on a landing page would improve donor conversion. The test was run through Meta ads that featured a premium mug. The control ad linked to a general donation page, while the test ad linked to a simplified landing page that included an image of the mug but removed fields and copy related to premiums.

experiments.title: Does a visually disruptive donation ask generate more donations than a contextually integrated one?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that placing the donation ask at the end of the article, with visual contrast and a gift array, would increase donor conversion because users would be more likely to consider donating after consuming the article content and be more inclined to act with a clearer path to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/26/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the effectiveness of in-article versus end-of-article donation asks on article pages. The “Dear Reader” style messages were designed to prompt donations in a low-friction, editorial-style format.

experiments.title: Does focusing on a physical premium on the donation page increase conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that by referencing the downloaded content offer and leaning into the electronic versus physical nature of the benefit, the test version would create a more seamless donor experience and increase conversion rates by making the Sustainer benefit more prominent.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2024 - 11/09/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate the impact of tailored messaging on a content offer donation page for “The People’s Pharmacy.” The goal was to determine whether referencing the content of the offer and emphasizing the electronic nature of the benefit would enhance donor conversion rates by making the experience feel more seamless and highlighting the value of becoming a Sustainer.

experiments.title: Does adding a gift array to a dear-reader effect transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding a gift array to the “Dear Reader” message, instead of just a button, will result in more donations by providing clear giving options. This approach simplifies decision-making and appeals to a wider range of donors, encouraging both recurring contributions and one-time gifts.

This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina NPR station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The body copy on both dear-readers remained the same.

The experiment comparing the "Dear Reader" message to the "Dear Reader with Gift Array" approach showed that adding a gift array did not lead to improved performance in terms of donation rates or average gift amounts.

Recurring Donations:
Both approaches yielded the same conversion rate (0.01%), showing no advantage for the gift array.

The gift array had a 27.4% lower conversion rate than the standard message, with a 51.0% confidence level.

Key Takeaway:

experiments.title: Which type of language (community membership or preserving free access) results in more donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that Variant 1 will result in more donations because it emphasizes the tangible perks and thank-you gifts that donors receive. The WUNC audience is often motivated by rewards and incentives, and offering gifts or exclusive benefits may create a stronger sense of immediate value and gratification.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/31/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, during its year-end fundraising campaign. The goal was to test which type of messaging language would make website visitors more likely to donate. Both messaging approaches were presented as slide-up prompts on the homepage of WUNC’s website.
Variant 1: Joining a Club
This approach emphasized becoming a Sustainer—a donor who gives regularly and enjoys special perks, such as thank-you gifts and prize entries. It targeted individuals who want to feel like long-term supporters of the station and value a sense of belonging to a community.
Variant 2: Keeping It Free
This approach focused on preserving WUNC’s free, paywall-free access to news and programming. It highlighted the station’s reliance on listener donations to remain freely available. The messaging aimed to appeal to individuals who value open access and might be motivated by gratitude to make a one-time contribution.
By testing these two distinct strategies, the experiment sought to identify which type of language—community membership or preserving free access—resonated more with potential donors and encouraged greater contributions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on the results of this experiment, Variant 1 (Joining a Club) outperformed Variant 2 (Keeping It Free) in terms of overall donation conversion rates. Variant 1 achieved a 2.6% conversion rate compared to Variant 2's 2.4% conversion rate, representing a 5.7% higher performance.
While Variant 2 emphasized preserving free access to WUNC's content, Variant 1's focus on perks and thank-you gifts appeared to resonate more with website visitors, leading to slightly higher donation rates. This suggests that offering tangible rewards and emphasizing membership benefits may be more effective at driving immediate contributions than messaging centered around maintaining free access.
Note: Although Variant 2 reportedly generated more recurring donations, this data is not validated and cannot be factored into the conclusions drawn from this experiment. Further research may be required to evaluate long-term donor retention strategies.

experiments.title: How adding additional value prop on a donation page impacts donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop to a donation form for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2024 - 11/11/2024
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. For this experiment, we wanted to see whether adding some additional value prop, especially around becoming a sustainer, impacted donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value prop to a donation form for potential donors showed a positive impact on donor conversions, with a noticeable increase in Transactions for All Traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group.

However, the statistical level of confidence of 27.6% is below the recommended threshold of 95%, indicating that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure a sufficient sample size and statistical power to generate reliable results. Additionally, clear and measurable key performance indicators should be established to accurately assess the impact of any changes or interventions. It may also be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to capture more data and variability in donor behavior.

Overall, while the initial findings suggest a potential benefit from adding value prop to donation forms, further experimentation with a stronger design and methodology is needed to draw meaningful conclusions and make informed decisions for future fundraising strategies.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts active donors during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional reinforcement for active donors will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment, which included additional reinforcement about WUNC and its purpose, resulted in a 16.0% decrease in click-through rates compared to the control group.

2. The statistical confidence level of 44.6% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid for decision-making.

3. Moreover, the treatment version had a 50% decrease in donations.

This experiment shows us, based on a small sample size, that active donors to WUNC do not need to be reminded about what WUNC is and does to be motivated to donate.

experiments.title: How adding a value prop reinforcement at the bottom of an email impacts prospects from a political newsletter list during a fund drive
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional value prop for political newsletter prospects will achieve a change in email clicks and conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2024 - 11/13/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station. During their Fall Fund Drive, we wanted to see if adding an additional call-out reinforcing what WUNC is and why people should donate to it during the drive, in an email, would impact conversion rates. This specific experiment targeted prospects that had signed up for a political newsletter before the elections.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional call-out reinforcing the value proposition of WUNC did not have the intended effect on click-through rates and conversion rates during the Fall Fund Drive. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group.

Moreover, the donation conversion rate was also lower for the treatment option, with a 33% decrease in conversions and a 35% level of confidence. However, the sample size was extremely small (only 5 total donations).

This test bears refinement and repeating with bigger audience sizes.

experiments.title: How an instructional headline effects email acquisitions
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that the instructional headline will lead to increased email acquisitions because it clearly communicates the benefits prospects will gain from signing up for the series, rather than simply stating the name of the series.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2024 - 10/22/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, focusing on a content offer landing page designed to encourage prospects to sign up for an email series aimed at helping them become more informed as the 2024 elections approach in North Carolina. We aimed to test different headlines to determine whether a more instructional would lead to higher email acquisition rates. The control group featured only the title of the email series.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment remain inconclusive due to a low statistical significance level of 25.8%. While the treatment group, which featured an instructional headline, experienced a slight decrease in email acquisitions compared to the control group, this difference cannot be considered statistically significant.

We also observed a marginally higher average gift, but this finding is similarly inconclusive. This experiment serves as a useful starting point; however, our focus should shift to optimizing the body copy of the landing page to assess its potential impact on results. Currently, it appears that neither headline significantly influenced whether prospects opted to sign up for the series.

Given these findings, it is premature to draw any definitive conclusions or use these results for future decision-making. For subsequent experiments, it is crucial to ensure an adequate sample size to yield reliable and meaningful results. Additionally, testing different variations of the action-oriented headline may provide insights that could lead to a more significant impact on email acquisitions.

experiments.title: Humanized vs. Premium: Which Slide-Out Drives More Transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that a slide-out showcasing a premium offer accompanied by a photo will drive more donations. This is based on the belief that prospects will be more inclined to donate when enticed by an attractive offer they can potentially obtain.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2024 - 08/30/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, sought to evaluate whether a slide-out featuring a compelling value proposition and photo or one highlighting a premium offer and photo would generate more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the slide-out showcasing a premium offer accompanied by a photo did not drive more donations compared to the slide-out featuring a compelling value proposition and photo. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 29.4% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control group outperformed the treatment group.
However, the statistical level of confidence for these results is only 77.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to increase the statistical power of the results. Additionally, it may be helpful to consider other factors that could have influenced the outcome of the experiment, such as the timing of the experiment or the specific wording and design of the slide-outs.
Overall, while this particular experiment did not yield definitive results, it provides valuable insights for future testing and optimization of donation strategies.

experiments.title: How humanizing an Anti-Paywall pop-up can effect donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We anticipate that integrating an anti-paywall pop-up onto the WUNC homepage will inspire prospects to consider donating. By presenting them with a clear value proposition by an actual person and reminding them of WUNC’s commitment to providing free content, we aim to instill a sense of reciprocity and encourage support for the station.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2024 - 07/25/2024

experiments.title: Do prospects respond better to a content offer or direct ask?
experiments.researchQuestion: By leveraging the appeal of exclusive content related to the election series, we anticipate capturing the interest of a broader audience segment and ultimately converting them into donors through a more gradual and engaging approach.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2024 - 05/03/2024
In collaboration with WUNC, a North Carolinian Radio Station, we initiated an experiment during their Spring Fund Drive to gauge the effectiveness of different approaches in soliciting donations. The core question revolved around whether a direct appeal or a more indirect approach through content offers would be more compelling to potential donors.
To execute this experiment, we implemented an “exit-intent” pop-up strategy. This feature activates when a visitor hovers their mouse over the left or right corner of the webpage, signaling an intent to leave the site. We devised two variations of the exit-intent pop-up to test our hypothesis.
The first variant presents a straightforward donation request, providing a direct link to the main donation page. Conversely, the second variant offers access to exclusive content related to the election series.
Drawing from our experiences with similar experiments in other nonprofit organizations, we’ve observed a consistent trend: prospects are often more receptive to content offers compared to direct donation appeals, even during high-urgency campaign seasons.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering exclusive content related to a topic of interest (in this case, the election series) can potentially be more effective in soliciting donations compared to a direct donation appeal. Although the treatment group saw a 2.8% increase in transactions for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 22.6%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the level of statistical confidence when evaluating the results. While the initial findings are promising, they should not be used as a basis for definitive conclusions or decision-making. It would be beneficial to conduct further tests with a larger sample size or different variations to validate the effectiveness of content offers in donation solicitation. Additionally, exploring other factors such as the timing of the campaign, the specificity of the content offered, and the audience demographics could provide further insights for optimizing donation strategies.

experiments.title: How adding an image of a thank-you gift impacted donations during a one-day fund drive.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of a thank-you gift for fund drive donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/08/2024
WUNC is one of North Carolina’s largest NPR stations. Most of their fundraising online comes through fund drives, which they have every other month.
February’s fund drive features some great thank you gifts, which we’ve seen through past testing help motivate donors to give to support WUNC. On a specific day during this drive, WUNC offered a box of chocolates and a WUNC-branded mug as the thank you gift, delivered in time for Valentine’s Day. This was a one-day only offer, so we wanted to test whether adding an image of the gift on the donation page made a difference in conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of a thank-you gift for fund drive donors resulted in a 13.6% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 33.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and improve the reliability of the results. This will help in making more informed decisions based on the outcomes of the experiments conducted.
